(**CD**) is a [digital](/wiki/Digital_media "Digital media") [optical disc](/wiki/Optical_disc "Optical disc") [data storage](/wiki/Data_storage "Data storage") format co-developed by [Philips](/wiki/Philips "Philips") and [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony") to store and play [digital audio](/wiki/Digital_audio "Digital audio") recordings. It employs the [Compact Disc Digital Audio](/wiki/Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio "Compact Disc Digital Audio") (CD-DA) standard and is capable of holding uncompressed [stereo](/wiki/Stereo "Stereo") audio. First released in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") in October 1982, the CD was the second optical disc format to reach the market, following the larger [LaserDisc](/wiki/LaserDisc "LaserDisc") (LD). In later years, the technology was adapted for computer data storage as [CD-ROM](/wiki/CD-ROM "CD-ROM") and subsequently expanded into various writable and multimedia formats. As of 2007[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Compact_disc&action=edit), over 200 billion CDs (including audio CDs, CD-ROMs, and [CD-Rs](/wiki/CD-R "CD-R")) had been sold worldwide.

The **compact disc** (**CD**) is a [digital](/wiki/Digital_media "Digital media") [optical disc](/wiki/Optical_disc "Optical disc") [data storage](/wiki/Data_storage "Data storage") format co-developed by [Philips](/wiki/Philips "Philips") and [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony") to store and play [digital audio](/wiki/Digital_audio "Digital audio") recordings. It employs the [Compact Disc Digital Audio](/wiki/Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio "Compact Disc Digital Audio") (CD-DA) standard and is capable of holding uncompressed [stereo](/wiki/Stereo "Stereo") audio. First released in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") in October 1982, the CD was the second optical disc format to reach the market, following the larger [LaserDisc](/wiki/LaserDisc "LaserDisc") (LD). In later years, the technology was adapted for computer data storage as [CD-ROM](/wiki/CD-ROM "CD-ROM") and subsequently expanded into various writable and multimedia formats. As of 2007[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Compact_disc&action=edit), over 200 billion CDs (including audio CDs, CD-ROMs, and [CD-Rs](/wiki/CD-R "CD-R")) had been sold worldwide.

Standard CDs have a diameter of 120 mm (4.7 in) and typically hold up to 74 minutes of audio or approximately 650 [MiB](/wiki/Mebibyte "Mebibyte") (681,574,400 bytes) of data. This was later regularly extended to 80 minutes or 700 MiB (734,003,200 bytes) by reducing the spacing between data tracks, with some discs unofficially reaching up to 99 minutes or 870 MiB (912,261,120 bytes) which falls outside established specifications. Smaller variants, such as the [Mini CD](/wiki/Mini_CD "Mini CD"), range from 60 to 80 mm (2.4 to 3.1 in) in diameter and have been used for [CD singles](/wiki/CD_single "CD single") or distributing [device drivers](/wiki/Device_driver "Device driver") and software.

The CD gained widespread popularity in the late 1980s and early 1990s. By 1991, it had surpassed the [phonograph record](/wiki/Phonograph_record "Phonograph record") and the [cassette tape](/wiki/Cassette_tape "Cassette tape") in sales in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), becoming the dominant physical audio format. By 2000, CDs accounted for 92.3% of the U.S. music market share.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The CD is widely regarded as the final dominant format of the [album era](/wiki/Album_era "Album era"), before the rise of [MP3](/wiki/MP3 "MP3"), [digital downloads](/wiki/Music_download "Music download"), and [streaming platforms](/wiki/Music_streaming_service "Music streaming service") in the mid-2000s led to its decline.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Beyond audio playback, the compact disc was adapted for general-purpose data storage under the [CD-ROM](/wiki/CD-ROM "CD-ROM") format, which initially offered more capacity than contemporary personal computer [hard disk drives](/wiki/Hard_disk_drive "Hard disk drive"). Additional derived formats include write-once discs ([CD-R](/wiki/CD-R "CD-R")), rewritable media ([CD-RW](/wiki/CD-RW "CD-RW")), and multimedia applications such as [Video CD](/wiki/Video_CD "Video CD") (VCD), [Super Video CD](/wiki/Super_Video_CD "Super Video CD") (SVCD), [Photo CD](/wiki/Photo_CD "Photo CD"), [Picture CD](/wiki/Picture_CD "Picture CD"), [Compact Disc Interactive](/wiki/Compact_Disc_Interactive "Compact Disc Interactive") (CD-i), [Enhanced Music CD](/wiki/Enhanced_Music_CD "Enhanced Music CD"), and [Super Audio CD](/wiki/Super_Audio_CD "Super Audio CD") (SACD), the latter of which can include a standard CD-DA layer for [backward compatibility](/wiki/Backward_compatibility "Backward compatibility").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Compact_disc&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

This section is an excerpt from [Compact Disc Digital Audio § History](/wiki/Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio#History "Compact Disc Digital Audio").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio&action=edit)]

The [optophone](/wiki/Optophone "Optophone"), first presented in 1913, was an early device that used light for both recording and playback of sound signals on a [transparent photograph](/wiki/Negative_(photography) "Negative (photography)").[[4]](#cite_note-4) More than thirty years later, American inventor [James T. Russell](/wiki/James_Russell_(inventor) "James Russell (inventor)") has been credited with inventing the first system to record digital media on a photosensitive plate. Russell's patent application was filed in 1966, and he was granted a patent in 1970.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Following litigation, [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony") and [Philips](/wiki/Philips "Philips") licensed Russell's patents for recording in 1988.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) It is debatable whether Russell's concepts, patents, and prototypes instigated and in some measure influenced the compact disc's design.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The compact disc is an evolution of [LaserDisc](/wiki/LaserDisc "LaserDisc") technology,[[9]](#cite_note-Immink-9) where a focused [laser](/wiki/Laser "Laser") beam is used that enables the high information density required for high-quality digital audio signals. Unlike the prior art by Optophonie and James Russell, the information on the disc is read from a reflective layer using a laser as a light source through a protective substrate. Prototypes were developed by Philips and Sony independently in the late 1970s.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Although originally dismissed by [Philips Research](/wiki/Philips_Research "Philips Research") management as a trivial pursuit,[[11]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_Immink2-11) the CD became the primary focus for Philips as the [LaserDisc](/wiki/LaserDisc "LaserDisc") format struggled.[[12]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_AndItsEnd-12)

In 1979, Sony and Philips set up a joint task force of engineers to design a new digital audio disc. The group of experts analyzed every detail of the proposed CD system and met every two months alternating between [Eindhoven](/wiki/Eindhoven "Eindhoven") and [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo") for discussions. Each time, the experiments conducted were discussed and the best solution was chosen from the prototypes developed by Sony and Philips. After experimentation, the group decided to adopt Sony’s error correction system, CIRC. [Immink](/wiki/Kees_Schouhamer_Immink "Kees Schouhamer Immink"), in a few months' time, developed the recording code called [eight-to-fourteen modulation](/wiki/Eight-to-fourteen_modulation "Eight-to-fourteen modulation") (EFM). EFM increases the playing time by more than 30% compared to the code used in the Philips prototype, without causing any issues with tracking. Sony and Philips decide to include EFM in the official Philips/Sony CD standard.[[11]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_Immink2-11) EFM and Sony’s error correction code, CIRC are the only standard [essential patents](/wiki/Essential_patent "Essential patent"), (SEP)s, of the compact disc.

After a year of experimentation and discussion, the *[Red Book](/wiki/Rainbow_Books "Rainbow Books")* CD-DA standard was published in 1980. After their commercial release in 1982, compact discs and their players were extremely popular. Despite costing up to $1,000, over 400,000 CD players were sold in the United States between 1983 and 1984.[[13]](#cite_note-13) By 1988, CD sales in the United States surpassed those of vinyl LPs, and, by 1992, CD sales surpassed those of prerecorded music-cassette tapes.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) The success of the compact disc has been credited to the cooperation between Philips and Sony, which together agreed upon and developed compatible hardware. The unified design of the compact disc allowed consumers to purchase any disc or player from any company and allowed the CD to dominate the at-home music market unchallenged.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### Digital audio laser-disc prototypes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Module:Excerpt&action=edit&section=T-1 "Edit section: Digital audio laser-disc prototypes")]

In 1974, Lou Ottens, director of the audio division of Philips, started a small group to develop an analog optical audio disc with a diameter of 20 cm (7.9 in) and a sound quality superior to that of the vinyl record.[[17]](#cite_note-17) However, due to the unsatisfactory performance of the analog format, two Philips research engineers recommended a digital format in March 1974. In 1977, Philips then established a laboratory with the mission of creating a digital audio disc. The diameter of Philips's prototype compact disc was set at 11.5 cm (4.5 in), the diagonal of an audio cassette.[[9]](#cite_note-Immink-9)[[18]](#cite_note-peek-18)

[Heitaro Nakajima](/wiki/Heitaro_Nakajima "Heitaro Nakajima"), who developed an early digital audio recorder within Japan's national public broadcasting organization, [NHK](/wiki/NHK "NHK"), in 1970, became general manager of Sony's audio department in 1971. In 1973, his team developed a digital [PCM adaptor](/wiki/PCM_adaptor "PCM adaptor") that made audio recordings using a [Betamax](/wiki/Betamax "Betamax") video recorder. After this, in 1974 the leap to storing digital audio on an optical disc was easily made.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Sony first publicly demonstrated an optical digital audio disc in September 1976. A year later, in September 1977, Sony showed the press a 30 cm (12 in) disc that could play an hour of digital audio (44,100 Hz sampling rate and 16-bit resolution) using [modified frequency modulation](/wiki/Modified_frequency_modulation "Modified frequency modulation") encoding.[[20]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_SonyHistorical-20)

In September 1978, Sony demonstrated an optical digital audio disc with a diameter of 30 cm (12 in) with a 150-minute playing time, 44,056 Hz sampling rate, 16-bit linear resolution, and [cross-interleaved Reed-Solomon coding](/wiki/Cross-interleaved_Reed-Solomon_coding "Cross-interleaved Reed-Solomon coding") (CIRC) [error correction code](/wiki/Error_correction_code "Error correction code")—specifications similar to those later settled upon for the standard compact disc format in 1980. Technical details of Sony's digital audio disc were presented during the 62nd [AES](/wiki/Audio_Engineering_Society "Audio Engineering Society") Convention, held on 13–16 March 1979, in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels").[[21]](#cite_note-SonyHistorical-21) Sony's AES technical paper was published on 1 March 1979. A week later, on 8 March, Philips publicly demonstrated a prototype of an optical digital audio disc at a press conference called "Philips Introduce Compact Disc"[[22]](#cite_note-BBC6950933-22) in [Eindhoven](/wiki/Eindhoven "Eindhoven"), Netherlands.[[23]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_PhilipsHistorical-23) Sony executive [Norio Ohga](/wiki/Norio_Ohga "Norio Ohga"), later CEO and chairman of Sony, and [Heitaro Nakajima](/wiki/Heitaro_Nakajima "Heitaro Nakajima") were convinced of the format's commercial potential and pushed further development despite widespread skepticism.[[24]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_Ohgaobituary-24)

### Collaboration and standardization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Module:Excerpt&action=edit&section=T-2 "Edit section: Collaboration and standardization")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Schouhamerimmink.jpg/250px-Schouhamerimmink.jpg)](/wiki/File:Schouhamerimmink.jpg)

Dutch inventor and Philips chief engineer [Kees Schouhamer Immink](/wiki/Kees_Schouhamer_Immink "Kees Schouhamer Immink") was part of the team that produced the standard compact disc in 1980

In 1979, Sony and Philips set up a joint task force of engineers to design a new digital audio disc. Led by engineers [Kees Schouhamer Immink](/wiki/Kees_Schouhamer_Immink "Kees Schouhamer Immink") and [Toshitada Doi](/wiki/Toshitada_Doi "Toshitada Doi"), the research pushed forward [laser](/wiki/Laser "Laser") and [optical disc](/wiki/Optical_disc "Optical disc") technology.[[25]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_BBC6950933-25) After a year of experimentation and discussion, the task force produced the *Red Book* CD-DA standard. First published in 1980, the standard was formally adopted by the [IEC](/wiki/IEC "IEC") as an international standard in 1987, with various amendments becoming part of the standard in 1996.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Philips coined the term *compact disc* in line with another audio product, the [Compact Cassette](/wiki/Compact_Cassette "Compact Cassette"),[[26]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_peek-26) and contributed the general manufacturing [process](/wiki/Industrial_process "Industrial process"), based on video LaserDisc technology. Philips also contributed [eight-to-fourteen modulation](/wiki/Eight-to-fourteen_modulation "Eight-to-fourteen modulation") (EFM), while Sony contributed the [error-correction](/wiki/Error-correction "Error-correction") method, CIRC, which offers resilience to defects such as scratches and fingerprints.

*The Compact Disc Story*,[[27]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_Immink-27) told by a former member of the task force, gives background information on the many technical decisions made, including the choice of the sampling frequency, playing time, and disc diameter. The task force consisted of around 6 persons,[[11]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_Immink2-11)[[28]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_Knopper-28) though according to Philips, the compact disc was "invented collectively by a large group of people working as a team".[[29]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_PhilDoss-29)

### Initial launch and adoption

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Module:Excerpt&action=edit&section=T-3 "Edit section: Initial launch and adoption")]

Early milestones in the launch and adoption of the format included:

* The first *test pressing* was of a recording of [Richard Strauss](/wiki/Richard_Strauss "Richard Strauss")'s *[An Alpine Symphony](/wiki/An_Alpine_Symphony "An Alpine Symphony")*, recorded December 1–3, 1980 and played by the [Berlin Philharmonic](/wiki/Berlin_Philharmonic "Berlin Philharmonic") and conducted by [Herbert von Karajan](/wiki/Herbert_von_Karajan "Herbert von Karajan"), who had been enlisted as an ambassador for the format in 1979.[[30]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_Karajan-30)
* The world presentation took place during the [Salzburg Easter Festival](/wiki/Salzburg_Easter_Festival "Salzburg Easter Festival") on 15 April 1981, at a press conference of [Akio Morita](/wiki/Akio_Morita "Akio Morita") and Norio Ohga (Sony), Joop van Tilburg (Philips), and Richard Busch (PolyGram), in the presence of Karajan who praised the new format.[[31]](#cite_note-31)
* The first *public demonstration* was on the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") television programme *[Tomorrow's World](/wiki/Tomorrow%27s_World "Tomorrow's World")* in 1981, when the [Bee Gees](/wiki/Bee_Gees "Bee Gees")' album *[Living Eyes](/wiki/Living_Eyes_(Bee_Gees_album) "Living Eyes (Bee Gees album)")* (1981) was played.[[32]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_AutoMR-4-32)
* The first *commercial* compact disc was produced on 17 August 1982, a 1979 recording of [Chopin](/wiki/Chopin "Chopin") waltzes performed by [Claudio Arrau](/wiki/Claudio_Arrau "Claudio Arrau").[[33]](#cite_note-33)
* The first 50 titles were *released* in Japan on 1 October 1982,[[34]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_AutoMR-6-34) the first of which was a re-release of [Billy Joel](/wiki/Billy_Joel "Billy Joel")'s 1978 album *[52nd Street](/wiki/52nd_Street_(album) "52nd Street (album)")*.[[35]](#cite_note-35)
* The first CD played on BBC Radio was in October 1982.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* The Japanese launch was followed on 14 March 1983 by the introduction of CD players and discs to Europe[[36]](#cite_note-36) and North America where CBS Records released sixteen titles.[[37]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_G&M_1983-03-05-37)

The first artist to sell a million copies on CD was [Dire Straits](/wiki/Dire_Straits "Dire Straits"), with their 1985 album *[Brothers in Arms](/wiki/Brothers_in_Arms_(album) "Brothers in Arms (album)")*.[[38]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_AutoMR-7-38) One of the first CD markets was devoted to reissuing popular music whose commercial potential was already proven. The first major artist to have their entire catalog converted to CD was [David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie"), whose first fourteen studio albums (up to *[Scary Monsters (and Super Creeps)](/wiki/Scary_Monsters_(and_Super_Creeps) "Scary Monsters (and Super Creeps)")*) of (then) sixteen were made available by [RCA Records](/wiki/RCA_Records "RCA Records") in February 1985, along with four greatest hits albums; his fifteenth and sixteenth albums (*[Let's Dance](/wiki/Let%27s_Dance_(David_Bowie_album) "Let's Dance (David Bowie album)")* and *[Tonight](/wiki/Tonight_(David_Bowie_album) "Tonight (David Bowie album)")*, respectively) had already been issued on CD by [EMI Records](/wiki/EMI_Records "EMI Records") in 1983 and 1984, respectively.[[39]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_AutoMR-8-39) On 26 February 1987, the first four UK albums by [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles") were released in mono on compact disc.[[40]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_BeatlesCD-40)

The growing acceptance of the CD in 1983 marked the beginning of the popular digital audio revolution.[[41]](#cite_note-41) It was enthusiastically received, especially in the early-adopting [classical music](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music") and [audiophile](/wiki/Audiophile "Audiophile") communities, and its handling quality received particular praise. As the price of players gradually came down, and with the introduction of the portable [Discman](/wiki/Discman "Discman"), the CD began to gain popularity in the larger popular and rock music markets. With the rise in CD sales, pre-recorded [cassette tape](/wiki/Cassette_tape "Cassette tape") sales began to decline in the late 1980s; CD sales overtook cassette sales in the early 1990s.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43) In 1988, 400 million CDs were manufactured by 50 pressing plants around the world.[[44]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_AutoMR-9-44)

### Further development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Module:Excerpt&action=edit&section=T-4 "Edit section: Further development")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Sony-Discman-D-E307CK.jpg/250px-Sony-Discman-D-E307CK.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sony-Discman-D-E307CK.jpg)

Sony [Discman](/wiki/Discman "Discman") D-E307CK portable CD player with 1-bit DAC

Early CD players employed binary-weighted [digital-to-analog converters](/wiki/Digital-to-analog_converter "Digital-to-analog converter") (DAC), which contained individual electrical components for each bit of the DAC.[[45]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_stereophile-19890501-Willenswaard-45) Even when using high-precision components, this approach was prone to decoding errors.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*][[45]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_stereophile-19890501-Willenswaard-45) Another issue was [jitter](/wiki/Jitter "Jitter"), a time-related defect. Confronted with the instability of DACs, manufacturers initially turned to increasing the number of bits in the DAC and using several DACs per audio channel, averaging their outputs.[[45]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_stereophile-19890501-Willenswaard-45) This increased the cost of CD players but did not solve the core problem.

A breakthrough in the late 1980s culminated in the development of the [1-bit DAC](/wiki/Delta-sigma_modulation#Digital-to-analog_conversion "Delta-sigma modulation"), which converts high-resolution low-frequency digital input signal into a lower-resolution high-frequency signal that is mapped to voltages and then smoothed with an analog filter. The temporary use of a lower-resolution signal simplified circuit design and improved efficiency, which is why it became dominant in CD players starting from the early 1990s. Philips used a variation of this technique called [pulse-density modulation](/wiki/Pulse-density_modulation "Pulse-density modulation") (PDM),[[46]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_stereophile-198906-Atkinson-46) while Matsushita (now [Panasonic](/wiki/Panasonic "Panasonic")) chose [pulse-width modulation](/wiki/Pulse-width_modulation "Pulse-width modulation") (PWM), advertising it as MASH, which is an acronym derived from their patented Multi-stAge noiSe-sHaping PWM topology.[[45]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_stereophile-19890501-Willenswaard-45)

The CD was primarily planned as the successor to the [vinyl record](/wiki/Vinyl_record "Vinyl record") for playing music, rather than as a data storage medium. However, CDs have grown to encompass other applications. In 1983, following the CD's introduction, Immink and [Joseph Braat](/wiki/Joseph_Braat "Joseph Braat") presented the first experiments with erasable compact discs during the 73rd [AES Convention](/wiki/AES_Convention "AES Convention").[[47]](#cite_note-47) In June 1985, the computer-readable [CD-ROM](/wiki/CD-ROM "CD-ROM") (read-only memory) and, in 1990, recordable [CD-R](/wiki/CD-R "CD-R") discs were introduced.[[a]](#cite_note-48) Recordable CDs became an alternative to tape for recording and distributing music and could be duplicated without degradation in sound quality.

Other newer video formats, such as [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD") and [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray"), use the same physical geometry as CD, and most DVD and Blu-ray players are [backward compatible](/wiki/Backward_compatible "Backward compatible") with audio CDs.

### Peak

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Module:Excerpt&action=edit&section=T-5 "Edit section: Peak")]

CD sales in the United States peaked by 2000.[[48]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_statista-49) By the early 2000s, the CD player had largely replaced the [audio cassette](/wiki/Audio_cassette "Audio cassette") player as standard equipment in new automobiles, with 2010 being the final model year for any car in the United States to have a factory-equipped cassette player.[[49]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_nyt20120718-50)

Two new formats were marketed in the 2000s, designed as successors to the CD: the [Super Audio CD](/wiki/Super_Audio_CD "Super Audio CD") (SACD) and [DVD-Audio](/wiki/DVD-Audio "DVD-Audio"). However, neither of these was adopted, partly due to the increased popularity of [music downloads](/wiki/Music_download "Music download") and the apparent lack of audible improvements in audio quality to most human ears.[[50]](#cite_note-51) These effectively extended the CD's longevity in the music market.[[51]](#cite_note-52)

### Decline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Module:Excerpt&action=edit&section=T-6 "Edit section: Decline")]

With the advent and popularity of [Internet-based distribution](/wiki/Digital_distribution "Digital distribution") of files in [lossy-compressed](/wiki/Lossy_compression "Lossy compression") [audio formats](/wiki/Audio_format "Audio format") such as [MP3](/wiki/MP3 "MP3"), sales of CDs began to decline in the 2000s. For example, between 2000 and 2008, despite overall growth in music sales and one anomalous year of increase, major-label CD sales declined overall by 20%.[[52]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_AutoMR-11-53) Despite rapidly declining sales year-over-year, the pervasiveness of the technology lingered for a time, with companies placing CDs in pharmacies, supermarkets, and filling station convenience stores to target buyers less likely to be able to use Internet-based distribution.[[12]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_AndItsEnd-12)

In 2012, CDs and DVDs made up only 34% of music sales in the United States.[[53]](#cite_note-54) By 2015, only 24% of music in the United States was purchased on physical media, two thirds of this consisting of CDs;[[54]](#cite_note-55) however, in the same year in Japan, over 80% of music was bought on CDs and other physical formats.[[55]](#cite_note-56) In 2018, U.S. CD sales were 52 million units—less than 6% of the peak sales volume in 2000.[[48]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_statista-49) In the UK, 32 million units were sold, almost 100 million fewer than in 2008.[[56]](#cite_note-57) In 2018, [Best Buy](/wiki/Best_Buy "Best Buy") announced plans to decrease their focus on CD sales, however, while continuing to sell records, sales of which are growing during the [vinyl revival](/wiki/Vinyl_revival "Vinyl revival").[[57]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_bestverge-58)[[58]](#cite_note-59)[[59]](#cite_note-60)

During the 2010s, the increasing popularity of solid-state media and music streaming services caused automakers to remove automotive CD players in favor of [minijack](/wiki/Minijack "Minijack") auxiliary inputs, wired connections to USB devices and wireless [Bluetooth](/wiki/Bluetooth "Bluetooth") connections.[[60]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_Biersdorfer-61) Automakers viewed CD players as using up valuable space and taking up weight, which could be reallocated to more popular features, like large touchscreens.[[61]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_Ramey-62) By 2021, only [Lexus](/wiki/Lexus "Lexus") and [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors") were still including CD players as standard equipment with certain vehicles.[[61]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_Ramey-62)

### Current status

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Module:Excerpt&action=edit&section=T-7 "Edit section: Current status")]

CDs continued to be strong in some markets such as Japan where 132 million units were produced in 2019.[[62]](#cite_note-63)

The decline in CD sales has slowed in recent years; in 2021, CD sales increased in the US for the first time since 2004,[[63]](#cite_note-64) with [Axios](/wiki/Axios_(website) "Axios (website)") citing its rise to "young people who are finding they like hard copies of music in the digital age".[[64]](#cite_note-65) It came at the same time as both vinyl and cassette reached sales levels not seen in 30 years.[[65]](#cite_note-66) The [RIAA](/wiki/RIAA "RIAA") reported that CD revenue made a dip in 2022, before increasing again in 2023 and overtook downloading for the first time in over a decade.[[66]](#cite_note-67)

In the US, 33.4 million CD albums were sold in the year 2022.[[67]](#cite_note-68) In [France](/wiki/France "France") in 2023, 10.5 million CDs were sold, almost double that of vinyl, but both of them represented generated 12% each of the French music industry revenues.[[68]](#cite_note-69)

### Awards and accolades

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Module:Excerpt&action=edit&section=T-8 "Edit section: Awards and accolades")]

Sony and Philips received praise for the development of the compact disc from professional organizations. These awards include:

* Technical [Grammy Award](/wiki/Grammy_Award "Grammy Award") for Sony and Philips, 1998.[[69]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_grammy-70)
* [IEEE](/wiki/IEEE "IEEE") Milestone award, 2009, for Philips alone with the citation: "On 8 March 1979, N.V. Philips' Gloeilampenfabrieken demonstrated for the international press a Compact Disc Audio Player. The demonstration showed that it is possible by using digital optical recording and playback to reproduce audio signals with superb stereo quality. This research at Philips established the technical standard for digital optical recording systems."[[70]](#cite_note-Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio_IEEE_CD_Milestone-71)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/CD_layers.svg/250px-CD_layers.svg.png)](/wiki/File:CD_layers.svg)

Diagram of CD layers

1. A polycarbonate disc layer has the data encoded by using bumps.
2. A shiny layer reflects the laser.
3. A layer of lacquer protects the shiny layer.
4. Artwork is [screen printed](/wiki/Screen_printing "Screen printing") on the top of the disc.
5. A laser beam is reflected off the CD to a sensor, which converts it into electronic data.

A CD is made from 1.2-millimetre (0.047 in) thick, [polycarbonate](/wiki/Polycarbonate "Polycarbonate") plastic, and weighs 14–33 grams.[[71]](#cite_note-72) From the center outward, components are: the center spindle hole (15 mm), the first-transition area (clamping ring), the clamping area (stacking ring), the second-transition area (mirror band), the program (data) area, and the rim. The inner program area occupies a radius from 25 to 58 mm.

A thin layer of [aluminum](/wiki/Aluminum "Aluminum") or, more rarely, [gold](/wiki/Gold_compact_disc "Gold compact disc") is applied to the surface, making it reflective. The metal is protected by a film of lacquer normally [spin coated](/wiki/Spin_coated "Spin coated") directly on the reflective layer. The label is printed on the lacquer layer, usually by [screen printing](/wiki/Screen_printing "Screen printing") or [offset printing](/wiki/Offset_printing "Offset printing").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Compactdiscar.jpg/250px-Compactdiscar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Compactdiscar.jpg)

*Pits* and *lands* of a compact disc under a [microscope](/wiki/Microscope "Microscope")

CD data is represented as tiny indentations known as *pits*, encoded in a spiral track molded into the top of the polycarbonate layer. The areas between pits are known as *lands*. Each pit is approximately 100 [nm](/wiki/Nanometre "Nanometre") deep by 500 nm wide, and varies from 850 nm to 3.5 [μm](/wiki/%CE%9Cm "Μm") in length.[[72]](#cite_note-73) The distance between the windings (the *pitch*) is 1.6 μm (measured center-to-center, not between the edges).[[73]](#cite_note-74)[[74]](#cite_note-IEC908-75)[[75]](#cite_note-IEC10149-76)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Compact_disk_data_layer_2d_3d.PNG/250px-Compact_disk_data_layer_2d_3d.PNG)](/wiki/File:Compact_disk_data_layer_2d_3d.PNG)

Individual pits are visible on the micrometer scale.

When playing an audio CD, a motor within the CD player spins the disc to a scanning velocity of 1.2–1.4 m/s ([constant linear velocity](/wiki/Constant_linear_velocity "Constant linear velocity"), CLV)—equivalent to approximately 500 RPM at the inside of the disc, and approximately 200 RPM at the outside edge.[[76]](#cite_note-:0-77) The track on the CD begins at the inside and spirals outward so a disc played from beginning to end slows its rotation rate during playback.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Comparison_CD_DVD_HDDVD_BD.svg/500px-Comparison_CD_DVD_HDDVD_BD.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Comparison_CD_DVD_HDDVD_BD.svg)

Comparison of various optical storage media

The program area is 86.05 cm2 and the length of the recordable spiral is 86.05 cm2 / 1.6 μm = 5.38 km. With a scanning speed of 1.2 m/s, the playing time is 74 minutes or 650 MiB of data on a CD-ROM. A disc with data packed slightly more densely is tolerated by most players (though some old ones fail). Using a linear velocity of 1.2 m/s and a narrower track pitch of 1.5 μm increases the playing time to 80 minutes, and data capacity to 700 MiB. Even denser tracks are possible, with semi-standard 90 minute/800 MiB discs having 1.33 μm, and 99 minute/870 MiB having 1.26 μm,[[77]](#cite_note-78) but compatibility suffers as density increases.

[![This is a photomicrograph of the pits at the inner edge of a CD-ROM; 2-second exposure under visible fluorescent light.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/CD_Pits_at_6.25x_Magnification.jpg/250px-CD_Pits_at_6.25x_Magnification.jpg)](/wiki/File:CD_Pits_at_6.25x_Magnification.jpg)

The pits in a CD are 500 [nm](/wiki/Nanometre "Nanometre") wide, between 830 nm and 3,000 nm long and 150 nm deep.

A CD is read by focusing a 780 nm [wavelength](/wiki/Wavelength "Wavelength") ([near infrared](/wiki/Near_infrared "Near infrared")) [semiconductor laser](/wiki/Semiconductor_laser "Semiconductor laser") (early players used [He‍–‍Ne laser](/wiki/Helium%E2%80%93neon_laser "Helium–neon laser")[[78]](#cite_note-79)) through the bottom of the polycarbonate layer. The change in height between pits and lands results in a difference in the way the light is reflected. Because the pits are indented into the top layer of the disc and are read through the transparent polycarbonate base, the pits form bumps when read.[[79]](#cite_note-80) The laser hits the disc, casting a circle of light wider than the modulated spiral track reflecting partially from the lands and partially from the top of any bumps where they are present. As the laser passes over a pit (bump), its height means that the round trip path of the light reflected from its peak is 1/2 wavelength out of phase with the light reflected from the land around it. This is because the height of a bump is around 1/4 of the wavelength of the light used, so the light falls 1/4 out of phase before reflection and another 1/4 wavelength out of phase after reflection. This causes partial [cancellation](/wiki/Wave_interference "Wave interference") of the laser's reflection from the surface. By measuring the reflected intensity change with a [photodiode](/wiki/Photodiode "Photodiode"), a modulated signal is read back from the disc.[[76]](#cite_note-:0-77)

To accommodate the spiral pattern of data, the laser is placed on a mobile mechanism within the disc tray of any CD player. This mechanism typically takes the form of a sled that moves along a rail. The sled can be driven by a [worm gear](/wiki/Worm_gear "Worm gear") or [linear motor](/wiki/Linear_motor "Linear motor"). Where a worm gear is used, a second shorter-throw linear motor, in the form of a coil and magnet, makes fine position adjustments to track eccentricities in the disc at high speed. Some CD drives (particularly those manufactured by Philips during the 1980s and early 1990s) use a swing arm similar to that seen on a gramophone.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/CDM210_cd_laufwerk.jpg/250px-CDM210_cd_laufwerk.jpg)](/wiki/File:CDM210_cd_laufwerk.jpg)

Philips CDM210 CD Drive

The pits and lands do *not* directly represent the 0s and 1s of [binary data](/wiki/Binary_data "Binary data"). Instead, [non-return-to-zero, inverted](/wiki/Non-return-to-zero,_inverted "Non-return-to-zero, inverted") encoding is used: a change from either pit to land or land to pit indicates a 1, while no change indicates a series of 0s. There must be at least two, and no more than ten 0s between each 1, which is defined by the length of the pit. This, in turn, is decoded by reversing the [eight-to-fourteen modulation](/wiki/Eight-to-fourteen_modulation "Eight-to-fourteen modulation") used in mastering the disc, and then reversing the [cross-interleaved Reed–Solomon coding](/wiki/Cross-interleaved_Reed%E2%80%93Solomon_coding "Cross-interleaved Reed–Solomon coding"), finally revealing the raw data stored on the disc. These encoding techniques (defined in the *[Red Book](/wiki/Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio#Data_encoding "Compact Disc Digital Audio")*) were originally designed for [CD Digital Audio](/wiki/CD_Digital_Audio "CD Digital Audio"), but they later became a standard for almost all CD formats (such as [CD-ROM](/wiki/CD-ROM "CD-ROM")).

### Integrity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Compact_disc&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Integrity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/CD_AluminumDamage_DiffractionGrating.jpg/250px-CD_AluminumDamage_DiffractionGrating.jpg)](/wiki/File:CD_AluminumDamage_DiffractionGrating.jpg)

Microscopic damage to the reflective layer of a CD at 200× magnification

CDs are susceptible to damage during handling and from environmental exposure. Pits are much closer to the label side of a disc, enabling defects and contaminants on the clear side to be out of focus during playback. Consequently, CDs are more likely to suffer damage on the label side of the disc. Scratches on the clear side can be repaired by refilling them with similar refractive plastic or by careful polishing. The edges of CDs are sometimes incompletely sealed, allowing gases and liquids to enter the CD and corrode the metal reflective layer and/or interfere with the focus of the laser on the pits, a condition known as [disc rot](/wiki/Disc_rot "Disc rot").[[80]](#cite_note-clir-81) The fungus *[Geotrichum candidum](/wiki/Geotrichum_candidum "Geotrichum candidum")* has been found—under conditions of high heat and humidity—to consume the polycarbonate plastic and aluminium found in CDs.[[81]](#cite_note-82)[[82]](#cite_note-83)

The [data integrity](/wiki/Data_integrity "Data integrity") of compact discs can be measured using [surface error scanning](/wiki/Optical_Disc#Surface_error_scanning "Optical Disc"), which can measure the rates of different types of data errors, known as *C1*, *[C2](/wiki/C2_error "C2 error")*, *CU* and extended (finer-grain) error measurements known as *E11*, *E12*, *E21*, *E22*, *E31* and *E32*, of which higher rates indicate a possibly damaged or unclean data surface, low media quality, [deteriorating media](/wiki/Disc_rot "Disc rot") and [recordable media](/wiki/CD-R "CD-R") written to by a malfunctioning [CD writer](/wiki/CD_writer "CD writer").

Error scanning can reliably predict data losses caused by media deterioration. Support of error scanning differs between vendors and models of [optical disc drives](/wiki/Optical_disc_drive#Appliances "Optical disc drive"), and *extended* error scanning (known as *"advanced error scanning"* in [Nero DiscSpeed](/wiki/Nero_DiscSpeed "Nero DiscSpeed")) which reports the six aforementioned E-type errors has only been available on [Plextor](/wiki/Plextor#Optical_drives "Plextor") and some [BenQ](/wiki/BenQ "BenQ") optical drives so far, as of 2020.[[83]](#cite_note-gleitz-84)[[84]](#cite_note-qpx-g-85)

### Disc shapes and diameters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Compact_disc&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Disc shapes and diameters")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Comparison_disk_storage.svg/500px-Comparison_disk_storage.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Comparison_disk_storage.svg)

Comparison of several forms of disc storage showing tracks (not to scale); green denotes start and red denotes end.  
\* Some CD-R(W) and DVD-R(W)/DVD+R(W) recorders operate in ZCLV, CAA or CAV modes.

The digital data on a CD begins at the inside near the spindle hole and spirals outward toward the edge in a single track. The outward spiral allows adaptation to different-sized discs. Standard CDs are available in two sizes. By far, the most common is 120 millimetres (4.7 in) in diameter, with a 74-, 80, 90, or 99-minute audio capacity and a 650, 700, 800, or 870 MiB (737,280,000-byte) data capacity. Discs are 1.2 millimetres (0.047 in) thick, with a 15 millimetres (0.59 in) center hole. The size of the hole was chosen by Joop Sinjou and based on a Dutch 10-cent coin: a [dubbeltje](/wiki/Dubbeltje "Dubbeltje").[[85]](#cite_note-86) Philips/Sony patented the physical dimensions.[[86]](#cite_note-87)

The official Philips history says the capacity was specified by Sony executive [Norio Ohga](/wiki/Norio_Ohga "Norio Ohga") to be able to contain the entirety of [Beethoven's Ninth Symphony](/wiki/Beethoven%27s_Ninth_Symphony "Beethoven's Ninth Symphony") on one disc.[[87]](#cite_note-Ohgaobituary-88)
According to Philips chief engineer [Kees Immink](/wiki/Kees_Immink "Kees Immink"), this is a myth,[[88]](#cite_note-Imminknature-89) as the [EFM](/wiki/Eight-to-fourteen_modulation "Eight-to-fourteen modulation") code format had not yet been decided in December 1979, when the 120 mm size was adopted. The adoption of EFM in June 1980 allowed 30 percent more playing time that would have resulted in 97 minutes for 120 mm diameter or 74 minutes for a disc as small as 100 millimetres (3.9 in). Instead, the information density was lowered by 30 percent to keep the playing time at 74 minutes.[[89]](#cite_note-Immink2-90)[[90]](#cite_note-91) The 120 mm diameter has been adopted by subsequent formats, including [Super Audio CD](/wiki/Super_Audio_CD "Super Audio CD"), [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD"), [HD DVD](/wiki/HD_DVD "HD DVD"), and [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") Disc. The 80-millimetre (3.1 in) diameter discs ("[Mini CDs](/wiki/Mini_CD "Mini CD")") can hold up to 24 minutes of music or 210 MiB.

| Physical size | Audio capacity | CD-ROM data capacity | Definition |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 120 mm | 74–80 min | 650–700 [MB](/wiki/Megabyte "Megabyte") | Standard size |
| 80 mm | 21–24 min | 185–210 MB | Mini-CD size |
| 80×54 mm – 80×64 mm | ~6 min | 10–65 MB | [Business card](/wiki/Business_card "Business card") size |

### SHM-CD

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Compact_disc&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: SHM-CD")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/SHM-CD_Subline_logo.svg/250px-SHM-CD_Subline_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:SHM-CD_Subline_logo.svg)

Logo used for SHM-CDs

**SHM-CD** (short for *Super High Material Compact Disc*) is a variant of the Compact Disc, which replaces the [polycarbonate](/wiki/Polycarbonate "Polycarbonate") base with a proprietary material. This material was created during joint research by [Universal Music Japan](/wiki/Universal_Music_Japan "Universal Music Japan") and [JVC](/wiki/JVC "JVC") into manufacturing high-clarity [liquid-crystal displays](/wiki/Liquid-crystal_display "Liquid-crystal display").

SHM-CDs are fully compatible with all CD players since the difference in light refraction is not detected as an error. JVC claims that the greater fluidity and clarity of the material used for SHM-CDs results in a higher reading accuracy and improved sound quality.[[91]](#cite_note-92) However, since the CD-Audio format contains inherent [error correction](/wiki/Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio#Data_encoding "Compact Disc Digital Audio"), it is unclear whether a reduction in read errors would be great enough to produce an improved output.

### Audio CD

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Compact_disc&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Audio CD")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/CDP101a.jpg/250px-CDP101a.jpg)](/wiki/File:CDP101a.jpg)

[Sony CDP-101](/wiki/Sony_CDP-101 "Sony CDP-101") from 1982, the first commercially released [CD](/wiki/CD "CD") player for [consumers](/wiki/Consumer "Consumer")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/CD_player.jpg/250px-CD_player.jpg)](/wiki/File:CD_player.jpg)

Philips CD100 from 1983, the first commercially released CD player in the US and Europe

Main article: [Compact Disc Digital Audio](/wiki/Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio "Compact Disc Digital Audio")

The logical format of an audio CD (officially Compact Disc Digital Audio or CD-DA) is described in a document produced in 1980 by the format's joint creators, Sony and Philips.[[92]](#cite_note-93) The document is known colloquially as the *Red Book* [CD-DA](/wiki/CD-DA "CD-DA") after the color of its cover. The format is a two-channel 16-bit [PCM](/wiki/PCM "PCM") encoding at a [44.1 kHz](/wiki/44.1_kHz "44.1 kHz") [sampling rate](/wiki/Sampling_rate "Sampling rate") per channel. [Four-channel sound](/wiki/Four-channel_Compact_Disc_Digital_Audio "Four-channel Compact Disc Digital Audio") was to be an allowable option within the *Red Book* format, but has never been implemented. [Monaural](/wiki/Monaural "Monaural") audio has no existing standard on a *Red Book* CD; thus, the mono source material is usually presented as two identical channels in a standard *Red Book* stereo track (i.e., [mirrored mono](/wiki/Mono_sound#Mirrored_mono "Mono sound")); an [MP3 CD](/wiki/MP3_CD "MP3 CD") can have audio file formats with mono sound.

[CD-Text](/wiki/CD-Text "CD-Text") is an extension of the *Red Book* specification for an audio CD that allows for the storage of additional text information (e.g., album name, song name, artist) on a standards-compliant audio CD. The information is stored either in the [lead-in area](/wiki/Optical_disc_authoring "Optical disc authoring") of the CD, where there are roughly five kilobytes of space available or in the [subcode](/wiki/Subcode "Subcode") channels R to W on the disc, which can store about 31 megabytes.

[Compact Disc + Graphics](/wiki/Compact_Disc_%2B_Graphics "Compact Disc + Graphics") is a special audio compact disc that contains graphics data in addition to the audio data on the disc. The disc can be played on a regular audio CD player, but when played on a special CD+G player, it can output a graphics signal (typically, the CD+G player is hooked up to a television set or a computer monitor); these graphics are almost exclusively used to display lyrics on a television set for [karaoke](/wiki/Karaoke "Karaoke") performers to sing along with. The CD+G format takes advantage of the channels R through W. These six bits store the graphics information.

[CD + Extended Graphics](/wiki/CD_%2B_Extended_Graphics "CD + Extended Graphics") (CD+EG, also known as CD+XG) is an improved variant of the [Compact Disc + Graphics](#CD_+_Graphics) (CD+G) format. Like CD+G, CD+EG uses basic CD-ROM features to display text and video information in addition to the music being played. This extra data is stored in subcode channels R-W. Very few CD+EG discs have been published.

### Super Audio CD

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Compact_disc&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Super Audio CD")]

Main article: [Super Audio CD](/wiki/Super_Audio_CD "Super Audio CD")

Super Audio CD (SACD) is a high-resolution, read-only [optical](/wiki/Optical_disc "Optical disc") [audio disc](/wiki/Audio_storage "Audio storage") format that was designed to provide [higher-fidelity](/wiki/High_fidelity "High fidelity") digital audio reproduction than the *Red Book*. Introduced in 1999, it was developed by Sony and Philips, the same companies that created the *Red Book*. SACD was in a [format war](/wiki/Format_war "Format war") with [DVD-Audio](/wiki/DVD-Audio "DVD-Audio"), but neither has replaced audio CDs. The SACD standard is referred to as the *Scarlet Book* standard.

Titles in the SACD format can be issued as hybrid discs; these discs contain the SACD audio stream as well as a standard audio CD layer which is playable in standard CD players, thus making them backward compatible.

### CD-MIDI

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Compact_disc&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: CD-MIDI")]

CD-[MIDI](/wiki/MIDI "MIDI") is a format used to store music-performance data, which upon playback is performed by electronic instruments that synthesize the audio. Hence, unlike the original *Red Book* CD-DA, these recordings are not digitally sampled audio recordings. The CD-MIDI format is defined as an extension of the original *Red Book*.

### CD-ROM

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Compact_disc&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: CD-ROM")]

Main article: [CD-ROM](/wiki/CD-ROM "CD-ROM")

For the first few years of its existence, the CD was a medium used purely for audio. In 1988, the *Yellow Book* [CD-ROM](/wiki/CD-ROM "CD-ROM") standard was established by Sony and Philips, which defined a non-volatile optical data [computer data storage](/wiki/Computer_data_storage "Computer data storage") medium using the same physical format as audio compact discs, readable by a computer with a CD-ROM drive.

### Video CD

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Compact_disc&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Video CD")]

Main article: [Video CD](/wiki/Video_CD "Video CD")

Video CD (VCD, View CD, and Compact Disc digital video) is a standard digital format for storing video media on a CD. VCDs are playable in dedicated VCD players, most modern [DVD-Video](/wiki/DVD-Video "DVD-Video") players, personal computers, and some video game consoles. The VCD standard was created in 1993 by Sony, Philips, [Matsushita](/wiki/Panasonic "Panasonic"), and [JVC](/wiki/JVC "JVC") and is referred to as the *White Book* standard.

Overall picture quality is intended to be comparable to [VHS](/wiki/VHS "VHS") video. Poorly compressed VCD video can sometimes be of lower quality than VHS video, but VCD exhibits block artifacts rather than analog noise and does not deteriorate further with each use. 352×240 (or [SIF](/wiki/Source_Input_Format "Source Input Format")) resolution was chosen because it is half the vertical and half the horizontal resolution of the NTSC video. 352×288 is a similarly one-quarter PAL/SECAM resolution. This approximates the (overall) resolution of an analog VHS tape, which, although it has double the number of (vertical) scan lines, has a much lower horizontal resolution.

### Super Video CD

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Compact_disc&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Super Video CD")]

Main article: [Super Video CD](/wiki/Super_Video_CD "Super Video CD")

Super Video CD (Super Video Compact Disc or SVCD) is a format used for storing video media on standard compact discs. SVCD was intended as a successor to VCD and an alternative to DVD-Video and falls somewhere between both in terms of technical capability and picture quality.

SVCD has two-thirds the [resolution](/wiki/Display_resolution "Display resolution") of DVD, and over 2.7 times the resolution of VCD. One CD-R disc can hold up to 60 minutes of standard-quality SVCD-format video. While no specific limit on SVCD video length is mandated by the specification, one must lower the video bit rate, and therefore quality, to accommodate very long videos. It is usually difficult to fit much more than 100 minutes of video onto one SVCD without incurring a significant quality loss, and many hardware players are unable to play a video with an instantaneous bit rate lower than 300 to 600 [kilobits](/wiki/Kilobit "Kilobit") per second.

### Photo CD

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Compact_disc&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Photo CD")]

Main article: [Photo CD](/wiki/Photo_CD "Photo CD")

Photo CD is a system designed by [Kodak](/wiki/Kodak "Kodak") for digitizing and storing photos on a CD. Launched in 1992, the discs were designed to hold nearly 100 high-quality images, scanned prints, and slides using special proprietary encoding. Photo CDs are defined in the *Beige Book* and conform to the [CD-ROM XA](/wiki/CD-ROM_XA "CD-ROM XA") and CD-i Bridge specifications as well. They are intended to play on CD-i players, Photo CD players, and any computer with suitable software (irrespective of [operating system](/wiki/Operating_system "Operating system")). The images can also be printed out on photographic paper with a special Kodak machine. This format is not to be confused with Kodak [Picture CD](/wiki/Picture_CD "Picture CD"), which is a consumer product in CD-ROM format.

### CD-i

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Compact_disc&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: CD-i")]

Main article: [Philips CD-i](/wiki/Philips_CD-i "Philips CD-i")

The Philips *[Green Book](/wiki/Green_Book_(CD_standard) "Green Book (CD standard)")* specifies a standard for interactive multimedia compact discs designed for [CD-i](/wiki/CD-i "CD-i") players (1993). CD-i discs can contain audio tracks that can be played on regular [CD players](/wiki/CD_player "CD player"), but CD-i discs are not compatible with most [CD-ROM](/wiki/CD-ROM "CD-ROM") drives and software. The [CD-i Ready](/wiki/CD-i_Ready "CD-i Ready") specification was later created to improve compatibility with audio CD players, and the [CD-i Bridge](/wiki/CD-i_Bridge "CD-i Bridge") specification was added to create CD-i-compatible discs that can be accessed by regular CD-ROM drives.

### CD-i Ready

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Compact_disc&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: CD-i Ready")]

Main article: [CD-i Ready](/wiki/CD-i_Ready "CD-i Ready")

Philips defined a format similar to CD-i called [CD-i Ready](/wiki/CD-i_Ready "CD-i Ready"), which puts CD-i software and data into the [pregap](/wiki/Pregap#Computer_data_in_pregap "Pregap") of track 1. This format was supposed to be more compatible with older audio CD players.

### Enhanced Music CD (CD+)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Compact_disc&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Enhanced Music CD (CD+)")]

Main article: [Blue Book (CD standard)](/wiki/Blue_Book_(CD_standard) "Blue Book (CD standard)")

Enhanced Music CD, also known as CD Extra or CD Plus, is a format that combines [audio tracks](/wiki/CD-DA "CD-DA") and [data tracks](/wiki/CD-ROM "CD-ROM") on the same disc by putting audio tracks in a first [session](/wiki/Session_(CD) "Session (CD)") and data in a second session. It was developed by Philips and Sony, and it is defined in the *[Blue Book](/wiki/Blue_Book_(CD_standard) "Blue Book (CD standard)")*.

### VinylDisc

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Compact_disc&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: VinylDisc")]

Main article: [VinylDisc](/wiki/VinylDisc "VinylDisc")

VinylDisc is the hybrid of a standard audio CD and the [vinyl record](/wiki/Gramophone_record "Gramophone record"). The vinyl layer on the disc's label side can hold approximately three minutes of music.

## Manufacture, cost, and pricing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Compact_disc&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Manufacture, cost, and pricing")]

Main article: [Compact Disc manufacturing](/wiki/Compact_Disc_manufacturing "Compact Disc manufacturing")

In 1995, material costs were 30 cents for the jewel case and 10 to 15 cents for the CD. The wholesale cost of CDs was $0.75 to $1.15, while the typical retail price of a prerecorded music CD was $16.98.[[93]](#cite_note-cd_costs-94) On average, the store received 35 percent of the retail price, the record company 27 percent, the artist 16 percent, the manufacturer 13 percent, and the distributor 9 percent.[[93]](#cite_note-cd_costs-94) When [8-track cartridges](/wiki/8-track_cartridges "8-track cartridges"), [compact cassettes](/wiki/Compact_cassette "Compact cassette"), and CDs were introduced, each was marketed at a higher price than the format they succeeded, even though the cost to produce the media was reduced. This was done because the perceived value increased. This continued from [phonograph records](/wiki/Phonograph_record "Phonograph record") to CDs, but was broken when [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") marketed MP3s for $0.99, and albums for $9.99. The incremental cost, though, to produce an MP3 is negligible.[[94]](#cite_note-95)

## Writable compact discs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Compact_disc&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Writable compact discs")]

### Recordable CD

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Compact_disc&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Recordable CD")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Maxell_CD-R_700MB_40x_20040321.jpg/250px-Maxell_CD-R_700MB_40x_20040321.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maxell_CD-R_700MB_40x_20040321.jpg)

700 [MiB](/wiki/MiB "MiB") CD-R next to a [mechanical pencil](/wiki/Mechanical_pencil "Mechanical pencil") for scale

Main article: [CD-R](/wiki/CD-R "CD-R")

Recordable Compact Discs, [CD-Rs](/wiki/CD-R "CD-R"), are injection-molded with a blank data spiral. A photosensitive dye is then applied, after which the discs are metalized and lacquer-coated. The write laser of the [CD recorder](/wiki/CD_recorder "CD recorder") changes the color of the dye to allow the read laser of a standard [CD player](/wiki/CD_player "CD player") to see the data, just as it would with a standard stamped disc. The resulting discs can be read by most CD-ROM drives and played in most audio CD players. CD-Rs follow the *Orange Book* standard.

CD-R recordings are designed to be permanent. Over time, the dye's physical characteristics may change causing read errors and data loss until the reading device cannot recover with error correction methods. Errors can be predicted using [surface error scanning](/wiki/Optical_disc#Surface_error_scanning "Optical disc"). The design life is from 20 to 100 years, depending on the quality of the discs, the quality of the writing drive, and storage conditions.[[95]](#cite_note-96) Testing has demonstrated such degradation of some discs in as little as 18 months under normal storage conditions.[[96]](#cite_note-AutoMR-14-97)[[97]](#cite_note-AutoMR-15-98) This failure is known as [disc rot](/wiki/Disc_rot "Disc rot"), for which there are several, mostly environmental, reasons.[[98]](#cite_note-99)

The recordable audio CD is designed to be used in a consumer audio CD recorder. These consumer audio CD recorders use SCMS ([Serial Copy Management System](/wiki/Serial_Copy_Management_System "Serial Copy Management System")), an early form of [digital rights management](/wiki/Digital_rights_management "Digital rights management") (DRM), to conform to the AHRA ([Audio Home Recording Act](/wiki/Audio_Home_Recording_Act "Audio Home Recording Act")). The Recordable Audio CD is typically somewhat more expensive than CD-R due to lower production volume and a 3 percent [AHRA royalty](/wiki/Audio_Home_Recording_Act#AHRA_royalties "Audio Home Recording Act") used to compensate the music industry for the making of a copy.[[99]](#cite_note-McFadden-100)

High-capacity recordable CD is a higher-density recording format that can hold 20% more data than conventional discs.[[100]](#cite_note-101) The higher capacity is incompatible with some recorders and recording software.[[101]](#cite_note-cdrfaq-102)

### ReWritable CD

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Compact_disc&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: ReWritable CD")]

Main article: [CD-RW](/wiki/CD-RW "CD-RW")

[CD-RW](/wiki/CD-RW "CD-RW") is a re-recordable medium that uses a metallic alloy instead of a dye. The write laser, in this case, is used to heat and alter the properties (amorphous vs. crystalline) of the alloy, and hence change its reflectivity. A CD-RW does not have as great a difference in reflectivity as a pressed CD or a CD-R, and so many earlier CD audio players cannot read CD-RW discs, although most later CD audio players and stand-alone [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD") players can. CD-RWs follow the *Orange Book* standard.

The ReWritable Audio CD is designed to be used in a consumer audio CD recorder, which will not (without modification) accept standard CD-RW discs. These consumer audio CD recorders use the [Serial Copy Management System](/wiki/Serial_Copy_Management_System "Serial Copy Management System") (SCMS), an early form of [digital rights management](/wiki/Digital_rights_management "Digital rights management") (DRM), to conform to the United States' [Audio Home Recording Act](/wiki/Audio_Home_Recording_Act "Audio Home Recording Act") (AHRA). The ReWritable Audio CD is typically somewhat more expensive than CD-R due to (a) lower volume and (b) a 3 percent [AHRA royalty](/wiki/Audio_Home_Recording_Act#AHRA_royalties "Audio Home Recording Act") used to compensate the music industry for the making of a copy.[[99]](#cite_note-McFadden-100)

## Copy protection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Compact_disc&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Copy protection")]

Main article: [Compact Disc and DVD copy protection](/wiki/Compact_Disc_and_DVD_copy_protection "Compact Disc and DVD copy protection")

See also: [Sony BMG copy protection rootkit scandal](/wiki/Sony_BMG_copy_protection_rootkit_scandal "Sony BMG copy protection rootkit scandal")

The *Red Book* audio specification, except for a simple *anti-copy* statement in the subcode, does not include any [copy protection](/wiki/Copy_protection "Copy protection") mechanism. Known at least as early as 2001,[[102]](#cite_note-BadCDs-103) attempts were made by record companies to market *copy-protected* non-standard compact discs, which cannot be [ripped](/wiki/Ripping "Ripping"), or copied, to hard drives or easily converted to other formats (like [FLAC](/wiki/FLAC "FLAC"), [MP3](/wiki/MP3 "MP3") or [Vorbis](/wiki/Vorbis "Vorbis")). One major drawback to these copy-protected discs is that most will not play on either computer CD-ROM drives or some standalone CD players that use CD-ROM mechanisms. Philips has stated that such discs are not permitted to bear the trademarked *Compact Disc Digital Audio* logo because they violate the *Red Book* specifications. Numerous copy-protection systems have been countered by readily available, often free, software, or even by simply turning off automatic [AutoPlay](/wiki/AutoPlay "AutoPlay") to prevent the running of the DRM [executable](/wiki/Executable "Executable") program.
